title: Tapping for the Should Be Over It Pressure
subtitle: A five-minute tapping practice for grief that others think should have ended
excerpt: A five-minute tapping practice for when the external pressure to be over a loss is running louder than the grief itself. Use it when someone has implied the timeline has passed, or when you are applying the pressure to yourself.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on public-domain grief literature (disenfranchised grief and the concept of a socially imposed mourning timeline). The tapping framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT), in use since the mid-1990s.

practice.whenToUse: Use when someone has said 'aren't you over that yet' or when you have started saying it to yourself.

content.text: A five-minute tapping practice for grief that has been given a timeline it cannot keep. Use it when the external pressure to have moved on has become part of the weight of the grief itself.
New to tapping? Read
how EFT tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop (repeat x3)
Even though people think I should be over this by now and I am not, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I feel ashamed that I still feel this, and I am angry that I should feel ashamed, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though grief does not follow the timeline people set for it, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Tapping round
**Eyebrow:** People think I should be over it.
**Side of eye:** I am not over it.
**Under eye:** I feel ashamed of that.
**Under nose:** And angry that I should feel ashamed.
**Chin:** My grief has its own timeline.
**Collarbone:** I cannot make it finish faster to make others comfortable.
**Under arm:** It is still here because it is still here.
**Top of head:** I am carrying this at my own pace.
Reframe to positive
**Eyebrow:** My grief is not overdue. It is mine.
**Side of eye:** I am allowed to still feel this.
**Under eye:** Grief has no expiry date.
**Under nose:** The timeline belongs to me.
**Chin:** I do not owe anyone my recovery.
**Collarbone:** I am allowed to carry this for as long as it needs carrying.
**Under arm:** My grief is the measure of what I loved.
**Top of head:** I am moving at my own pace. That is enough.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from

Original to homemade.education, drawing on public-domain grief literature including Kenneth Doka's concept of disenfranchised grief (grief that society does not fully recognise or permit). The tapping framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT), developed in the mid-1990s.
